Topic: Red White  Royal Blue 2019 by Casey McQuiston
Key Fact: Casey McQuiston’s debut novel Red, White & Royal Blue  is a queer romantic comedy set in an alt-reality where the United States has its first female President. The central narrative follows Alex Claremont-Díaz, the bisexual First Son, and his evolving secret romance with Prince Henry of Wales. With vibrant humor, political stakes, and sharp dialogue, the novel celebrates queer love as transformative on both personal and international levels. Praised for its lively characters and emotional depth, the book has resonated widely with readers and critics alike and inspired a successful film adaptation in 2023.

Transgender issues are compellingly explored across Myself Mona Ahmed by Dayanita Singh, The Ministry of Utmost Happiness by Arundhati Roy, and The Man Who Would Be Queen by Hoshang Merchant, each offering distinct yet intersecting portrayals of gender nonconformity in the Indian sociocultural context. In Myself Mona Ahmed (2001), Singh documents the life of Mona Ahmed, a eunuch from Delhi, through an intimate photographic narrative that defies voyeurism and humanizes the subject beyond the traditional hijra archetype. Mona’s own voice, featured through emails and reflections, foregrounds themes of marginalization, longing, and resilience in a society that both excludes and exoticizes her identity. Roy’s The Ministry of Utmost Happiness (2017)  further expands this narrative through the character of Anjum, a transgender hijra who constructs her own inclusive sanctuary in a graveyard—symbolically turning a space of death into a domain of life, memory, and dissent. Anjum’s journey confronts the intersections of caste, gender, and communal violence, offering a politically charged yet deeply humane depiction of trans life. In The Man Who Would Be Queen (1999) , poet and critic Hoshang Merchant blends autobiography with poetic reflection, navigating the terrains of queer desire, effeminacy, and spiritual queerness in the context of Indian masculinity. Merchant’s lyrical explorations challenge normative constructions of gender and offer an intellectual, aesthetic lens into transgender and queer subjectivities. Together, these works provide a multifaceted discourse on the trans experience in India—marked by exclusion, self-articulation, resistance, and cultural reinvention.

Topic: Critical Explorations of Black Masculinity and Queer Identity
Key Fact: Philip Brian Harper’s Are We Not Men? (1996)  investigates constructions of Black masculinity in American culture, interrogating how racialized male bodies are shaped by social expectations of strength, heterosexuality, and stoicism. Harper critiques cultural narratives that restrict Black men to hypermasculine roles, proposing instead a broader framework that embraces vulnerability, complexity, and queerness. Erica R. Edwards’s Charisma and the Fictions of Black Leadership (2012) examines the literary and political figure of the charismatic Black male leader—from Martin Luther King Jr. to Barack Obama—and how this model often marginalizes Black women and queer voices. Edwards critiques the overrepresentation of masculine charisma in African American political imagination and argues for alternative modes of collective agency. Darieck Scott’s Extravagant Abjection: Blackness, Power, and Sexuality in the African American Literary Imagination (2010) reimagines Black queer sexuality not as a site of shame but as a radical space of resistance. Using writers like Baldwin and Morrison, Scott explores how abjection, pain, and queerness disrupt dominant cultural scripts and produce new modes of Black subjectivity. Together, these works challenge hegemonic notions of Black identity, offering expansive, intersectional frameworks that foreground queerness, power, and literary imagination.

Aristotle’s Rhetoric  is a foundational treatise on persuasion, composed in the fourth century BCE. Sometimes titled The Art of Rhetoric, it analyzes the techniques of effective argument and public speaking. Aristotle defines rhetoric as the capacity to see, in any given case, the available means of persuasion. He identifies three central appeals: ethos (the speaker’s credibility), pathos (emotional engagement of the audience), and logos (the logical structure of the argument). The treatise further investigates how emotions influence judgment, how style and delivery affect reception, and how reasoned speech shapes civic and ethical life. Rhetoric thus stands as a key text not only in classical oratory but also in the ongoing study of communication.

Aristotle’s Metaphysics  is regarded as the cornerstone of philosophical inquiry into “being as being.” This monumental work explores substance theory, causation, and the relationship of form and matter. It also engages questions of universals, the existence of mathematical objects, and the nature of the cosmos. By investigating the first principles and causes of reality, Aristotle inaugurates what he calls “First Philosophy.” The Metaphysics assembles and refines many of his most abstract ideas, providing a framework that has shaped centuries of metaphysical debate from late antiquity through medieval scholasticism and into modern philosophy.

The Organon is a collection of six works that establish Aristotle’s logic as the “instrument” (organon in Greek) of philosophical inquiry. Compiled by his Peripatetic followers, the collection includes treatises on categories, interpretation, prior and posterior analytics, topics, and sophistical refutations. Here Aristotle systematizes both deductive reasoning (syllogism) and inductive reasoning, setting the foundation for formal logic. His logical method would influence medieval scholastics and inspire early modern thinkers such as Francis Bacon, whose Novum Organum was a deliberate response to Aristotle’s framework. It inspired Bacon’s The Novum Organum .

Topic: A Treatise of Human Nature 17391740 Naturalistic Science of Man
Key Fact: Hume’s magnum opus, A Treatise of Human Nature (1739–1740), represents his first major attempt to build a systematic philosophy. A Treatise of Human Nature (1739–40) . Though he later lamented that the work had “fell dead-born from the press,” the Treatise remains foundational for his thought. It is divided into three books. Book I, Of the Understanding, investigates the origin of ideas, the concepts of space and time, knowledge and probability, and the nature of causality, leading to profound skeptical conclusions. Book II, Of the Passions, develops a psychological account of human emotions, showing how affective responses govern conduct while reason plays only a secondary role. Book III, Of Morals, locates moral distinctions not in rational deduction but in sentiments of approval or disapproval that people experience when evaluating human actions in terms of their agreeable or disagreeable consequences. Together, the three books outline Hume’s naturalistic psychology, portraying human beings as governed less by rationality than by habit and feeling.

Topic: An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding 1748 Refined Empiricism
Key Fact: Disappointed by the reception of the Treatise, Hume reworked its central ideas into the more concise and accessible An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding (1748). An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding (1748) . This text distills his most provocative arguments, leaving aside personal identity but emphasizing the role of custom and habit in shaping belief. The Enquiry famously asserts that our idea of causality arises not from logical deduction but from repeated association: we expect events to follow one another because experience habituates us to that pattern. Its skeptical conclusions left a lasting impact on later philosophy, most notably awakening Immanuel Kant from what he described as his “dogmatic slumber.” The work unfolds incrementally, with sections on the species of philosophy, the origin and association of ideas, skeptical doubts and their solutions, probability, necessary connection, liberty and necessity, the reasoning of animals, miracles, providence, and the skeptical tradition. It remains a classic of modern philosophical literature.

Among Dante’s prose works, Convivio (The Banquet) is particularly notable, though unfinished. Structured into four treatises (trattati), each containing a long lyric poem (canzone) followed by allegorical prose commentary, the work sought to serve as a kind of vernacular encyclopedia of medieval knowledge. The opening proem justifies the project, explaining why Dante wrote in Italian rather than Latin. It is one of Dante’s early defenses of the vernacular , a theme he later elaborated more fully in his treatise On Eloquence in the Vernacular. In combining poetry with scholarly commentary, Convivio reflects Dante’s ambition to democratize learning and elevate the cultural role of the Italian language.

Topic: Giovanni Boccaccio 13131377 Storytelling Humanism and the Vernacular
Key Fact: Boccaccio’s Decameron is regarded as his greatest achievement and a landmark in European literature. The Decameron (composed in 1348–52, revised 1370–71) . Written during the devastation of the Black Death, the frame narrative follows ten young Florentines who retreat to the countryside at Fiesole to escape the plague. Over ten days, each tells one story daily, creating a collection of one hundred tales that encompass romance, comedy, tragedy, satire, and moral reflection. Each storytelling day closes with a canzone, integrating lyric poetry into the prose framework. Its blend of realism, wit, and moral insight influenced Geoffrey Chaucer’s Canterbury Tales as well as his Troilus and Criseyde, which drew inspiration from Boccaccio’s Il Filostrato (1338). Similarly, Boccaccio’s Teseida (1340–41), an epic in Italian, provided Chaucer with material for The Knight’s Tale.

Boccaccio’s literary output extended well beyond The Decameron. His L’amorosa Visione (The Amorous Vision, 1342, revised c. 1365) is a fifty-canto narrative poem composed in terza rima, offering allegorical reflections on love and virtue. Il ninfale d’Ameto (Ameto’s Story of the Nymphs, 1341–42) is a pastoral allegory that combines prose with nineteen cantos in terza rima, reflecting his early experiments with blending genres. His later years were marked by his engagement with Dante: between 1373 and 1374 he delivered public lectures on the Divine Comedy at the Santo Stefano church in Florence, producing a formal commentary on Dante’s Divine Comedy .

Topic: Thomas Campion 15671620 Music Poetry and the Defence of Verse
Key Fact: Thomas Campion, the English composer, poet, and physician, combined his training at Cambridge and Gray’s Inn with a career that bridged music and literature. Renowned for his more than one hundred lute songs and court masques, he also contributed to literary criticism through Observations in the Art of English Poesie (1602), where he argued “against the vulgar and unartificial custom of riming,” advocating instead for unrhymed verse modeled on classical quantitative patterns.  His bold challenge to English poetic convention provoked immediate response, most notably Samuel Daniel’s Defence of Rhyme (1603), which countered Campion’s call for innovation by defending rhyme as central to English verse.

Topic: Samuel Daniel 15621619 Poet Historian and Defender of Rhyme
Key Fact: Samuel Daniel, active during the late-Elizabethan and early-Jacobean periods, was a poet, playwright, and historian whose work spanned diverse genres. He is best remembered for his sonnet sequence Delia, the historical poem The Civil Wars Between the Houses of Lancaster and York, and the reflective dialogue in verse Musophilus. His critical contribution, A Defense of Rhyme,  stands as a notable prose treatise responding to Thomas Campion’s Observations in the Art of English Poesie (1602) . In this work, Daniel defended the legitimacy of English verse forms, rejecting the notion that poetry must adhere strictly to classical standards, and instead affirming the expressive capacity and cultural validity of rhyme in the vernacular tradition.

Topic: Richard Hooker 15541600 Ecclesiastical Order and Authority
Key Fact: Richard Hooker, a leading English theologian of the sixteenth century, is best remembered for his monumental work Ecclesiastical Polity. Ecclesiastical Polity . Written in defense of the Church of England against Puritan reformers who sought to dismantle its traditional structures, the work combines political philosophy with theology to argue for the legitimacy and necessity of the established church. Hooker maintained that the practices and hierarchical order of the Church were not arbitrary but essential for the spiritual health of the faithful and the stability of the state itself, making his text a cornerstone of Anglican thought and early modern political theology.

Among Milton’s prose works, Areopagitica (1644) remains his most enduring statement on intellectual and political freedom. Its full title—Areopagitica; A Speech of Mr. John Milton for the Liberty of Unlicenc’d Printing to the Parlament of England—underscores its nature as a passionate plea against pre-publication censorship. Written in response to the Licensing Order of 1643, which sought to control printed works, the tract denounced the suppression of knowledge and affirmed the necessity of open inquiry for the pursuit of truth. “Liberty of the Press” , drawn from Areopagitica, became a rallying phrase for freedom of speech and expression. Widely regarded as the most dynamic and persuasive defense of the free press in English literature, Areopagitica fused classical rhetoric with Puritan conviction, securing Milton’s place not only as a poet of sublime verse but also as a defender of liberty in its most radical form.

Topic: An Essay Concerning Human Understanding 1689 Foundations of Empiricism
Key Fact: Locke’s most celebrated philosophical treatise, An Essay Concerning Human Understanding (1689), remains a cornerstone of modern empiricism. John Locke: An Essay Concerning Human Understanding (1689) . In this work, Locke explicitly rejects the rationalist doctrine of innate ideas, insisting instead that all knowledge originates in sensory experience. Locke depicts the mind at birth as a blank slate or tabula rasa filled later through experience .

Essays on Aesthetics Standard of Taste and Tragedy
Key Fact: Hume’s essays extend his empiricism into aesthetics, where he examined questions of judgment and taste. Standard of Taste . In this essay, Hume acknowledged the difficulty—perhaps impossibility—of defining universal standards of beauty, yet he posited the existence of a reliable critic who, being experienced, objective, sensible, and free from prejudice, could serve as a measure of refined taste. Similarly, in Of Tragedy, Hume explored the paradox of why audiences derive pleasure from tragic drama. He explained that the awareness of fiction transforms feelings of sorrow and anxiety into a form of aesthetic enjoyment, offering insight into the psychology of spectatorship.

Topic: Chapter XIV The Cardinal Points of Poetry
Key Fact: Chapter XIV is among the most celebrated parts of Biographia Literaria. Here Coleridge defends Lyrical Ballads and articulates what he calls the “two cardinal points of poetry.” For Coleridge, the cardinal points of poetry are : first, the power to excite sympathy in the reader through a faithful adherence to nature’s truths; second, the ability to arouse novelty by coloring experience with the imagination. This dual principle was embodied in the design of Lyrical Ballads, where Wordsworth contributed poems rooted in ordinary life while Coleridge composed works exploring the supernatural. Coleridge insisted that these two approaches were not opposed but complementary, each requiring the active participation of the imagination to transform reality into art. While he supported Wordsworth’s poetic creed, particularly his use of the “language of real life,” Coleridge nonetheless disagreed with his rigid views on poetic diction. Yet, in this chapter, he came to Wordsworth’s defense against hostile critics, justifying the revolutionary experiment of Lyrical Ballads as a new model for poetry.

The term “Soft Capitalism” was coined by Nigel Thrift to describe a late twentieth-century form of capitalism that relies not solely on industrial production but on emotionally driven, consumer-oriented practices. Soft Capitalism refers to emotionally driven, consumer-oriented capitalism . This mode of capitalism focuses on lifestyle branding, affective labor, and symbolic consumption, where emotional appeal and cultural identity become central to economic exchange. In this framework, products are sold not just for utility but for the feelings, aspirations, and cultural meanings they embody, marking a shift from traditional “hard” industrial capitalism to a system sustained by desire and affect.

Topic: Paul J Crutzen 19332021 The Anthropocene and Atmospheric Chemistry
Key Fact: Paul Jozef Crutzen (December 3, 1933 – January 28, 2021) was a Dutch meteorologist and atmospheric chemist whose pioneering research transformed the understanding of Earth’s atmosphere. Awarded the 1995 Nobel Prize in Chemistry alongside Mario Molina and Frank Sherwood Rowland, Crutzen was recognized for his groundbreaking work on atmospheric chemistry, particularly the processes governing the formation and decomposition of the ozone layer. His contributions not only advanced knowledge of ozone depletion but also helped shape global environmental policies such as the Montreal Protocol. Beyond ozone studies, Crutzen became widely known for popularizing the term “Anthropocene” to describe a proposed epoch in which human activity exerts a decisive and potentially irreversible impact on the planet’s systems . He also played a key role in early discussions of nuclear winter, warning of the catastrophic climatic consequences of large-scale atmospheric pollution caused by industrial emissions, wildfires, and oil fires. Through his scientific vision and terminology, Crutzen reshaped how both science and the humanities conceptualize the human relationship to planetary change, making him a foundational figure in contemporary environmental thought.

Topic: Black Skin White Masks 1952 The Psychology of Racial Oppression
Key Fact: Frantz Fanon’s Black Skin, White Masks (1952) remains one of the most important early contributions to postcolonial theory and the psychology of race . In this groundbreaking work, Fanon examined the condition of the oppressed Black subject in a world structured by White domination. He argued that the colonized individual is compelled to navigate existence by adopting the “performance” of Whiteness, which simultaneously enables participation in the colonial order and deepens internalized alienation. The colonizer, however, perceives the Black person’s adoption of the colonizer’s language and cultural forms as predatory rather than transformative, a perception that breeds insecurity and fractures the consciousness of the colonized.

Topic: The Wretched of the Earth 1961 Violence and Decolonization
Key Fact: Fanon’s final work, The Wretched of the Earth (1961), published shortly before his death and famously prefaced by Jean-Paul Sartre, offered a searing manifesto for liberation . Censored by the French government, the book defended the colonized peoples’ right to employ violence in their struggles for independence. Fanon argued that colonialism itself is founded on violence—Algeria, for example, was subjugated through the sheer military strength of its colonizers. Therefore, resistance must also be violent, for it is the only “language” that the colonizer understands. According to Fanon, those who are denied humanity by the colonial order cannot be bound by its moral codes; violent struggle thus becomes both a necessity and a cathartic process of reclaiming dignity.

A concept central to Fanon’s work, double consciousness describes the internal conflict experienced by colonized or racially oppressed groups who view themselves both through their own cultural identity and through the degrading lens imposed by the dominant group. While Fanon developed this idea in the context of colonial alienation, its origins lie in W. E. B. Du Bois’s The Souls of Black Folk (1903), where Du Bois reflected on the African American experience of fractured selfhood . Fanon extended this concept to the colonial world, showing how the colonized subject becomes split between the desire for assimilation and the impossibility of escaping imposed otherness.

Key Fact: The concept of the Grand Narrative or Meta-Narrative  refers to an overarching framework that provides meaning to historical events, cultural experiences, or forms of knowledge. Such narratives offer societies a sense of legitimation by projecting the eventual fulfillment of a master idea, often tied to notions of progress, emancipation, or enlightenment. In the postmodern critique, however, these totalizing frameworks are dismantled as illusory, exposing their inability to account for the multiplicity of fragmented identities and cultural practices.

Alan Sinfield and Jonathan Dollimore made a landmark contribution to cultural materialism and Marxist literary criticism with their edited volume Political Shakespeare (1985). Political Shakespeare (1985), edited by Alan Sinfield and Jonathan Dollimore, is a landmark in cultural materialism and Marxist Shakespeare criticism . The book interrogated Shakespearean texts through the lenses of power, ideology, and history, challenging ahistorical readings of the plays. It foregrounded how literature operates within cultural and political frameworks, thereby redefining Shakespeare studies as a politically engaged field aligned with the principles of cultural studies.

In their seminal work How to Read Donald Duck (1971), Ariel Dorfman and Armand Mattelart exposed the hidden imperialist ideology embedded within Disney comics. Their critique focused on the tropes of “impoverishment” and the “noble savage” , showing how narratives of popular culture reinforced colonial stereotypes and legitimized global inequalities. By deconstructing seemingly innocent children’s entertainment, they revealed how mass media functions as a vehicle for cultural imperialism, reproducing capitalist and colonial ideologies under the guise of entertainment.

Michel de Certeau’s The Practice of Everyday Life (1984)  is a landmark text in cultural theory, emphasizing how ordinary people navigate, resist, and reinterpret dominant cultural systems in their daily lives. By focusing on everyday practices such as walking, reading, or shopping, de Certeau revealed the agency of individuals in negotiating cultural structures. His work shifted attention from macro-level ideology to the micro-practices of daily living, highlighting the creativity of ordinary cultural consumption.

Hazel Carby’s Reconstructing Womanhood (1987) is a seminal contribution to Black feminist literary criticism . The book analyzes the works of nineteenth-century African American women writers and explores how they redefined concepts of womanhood, race, and identity in ways that challenged both white feminist and patriarchal discourses. Carby’s work was central in legitimizing Black women’s literature within academia and in highlighting the intersections of race, gender, and class in cultural production.

Topic: Multiculturalism Diversity Recognition and Democratic Inclusion
Key Fact: One of the most notable examples of institutionalized multicultural policy is the Canadian Multiculturalism Act of 1988, which formally recognized Canada’s cultural diversity and codified respect for plural identities into national law . By embedding multicultural values into democratic institutions, the Act exemplified how states can move beyond tolerance toward an active embrace of cultural difference.

Topic: Francis Yoshihiro Fukuyama b 1952 Liberal Democracy and the End of History
Key Fact: Francis Yoshihiro Fukuyama, born October 27, 1952, is an American political scientist, political economist, and international relations scholar best known for his influential and controversial book The End of History and the Last Man (1992) . In this work, Fukuyama argued that the global spread of liberal democracy and free-market capitalism might represent the culmination of humanity’s sociocultural evolution and political struggle—the “final form” of government. This thesis, which suggested that ideological conflict had reached its endpoint after the Cold War, sparked widespread debate and criticism from across the political spectrum. Fukuyama refined his position in Trust: Social Virtues and the Creation of Prosperity (1995), where he emphasized that culture and social values cannot be separated from economics in sustaining prosperity. Although once associated with the rise of the neoconservative movement, particularly in the 1990s, Fukuyama later distanced himself from it, especially following the Iraq War. His work continues to influence global discussions on democracy, governance, and the tensions between universalist political models and cultural particularities.

Topic: Madan Sarup 1925  Poststructuralism Identity and Education
Key Fact: Madan Sarup, lecturer at Goldsmiths College, London, was a noted cultural theorist whose writings made complex ideas in poststructuralism, postmodernism, and critical theory accessible to students and scholars alike. Best known for his An Introductory Guide to Post-Structuralism and Postmodernism, Sarup mapped the intellectual terrain of modernity and postmodernity, while engaging critically with theorists such as Lacan, Foucault, Kristeva, Cixous, Irigaray, Lyotard, and Baudrillard. His work explored feminist critiques of psychoanalysis, the politics of language, and the cultural practices of postmodernism, including the destabilization of identity and meaning through narrative and difference. Beyond literary theory, Sarup contributed significantly to the sociology of education, particularly through a Marxist and phenomenological lens that interrogated alienation, labor processes, and the political economy of schooling. He also addressed questions of identity and culture in the postmodern world,  analyzing the intersections of race, ethnicity, consumer identity, and postcolonial criticism. In his writings on multiracial education, Sarup critiqued models grounded solely in psychology, equality of opportunity, or cultural pluralism, instead urging a deeper engagement with the structural and political dimensions of inequality. Through these contributions, Sarup bridged theoretical abstraction with pressing social concerns, shaping debates on education, culture, and identity in the late twentieth century.

This volume collects some of Derrida’s earliest lectures and essays that first brought him international recognition. Published in 1967 alongside Of Grammatology and Speech and Phenomena, it interrogates the philosophical and literary canon through close readings of figures such as Foucault, Bataille, and Levinas. Derrida shows how texts, when analyzed rigorously, tend to “betray themselves” — producing contradictions that undermine their own stated logic. The book exemplifies the deconstructive strategy of reading, where meaning is never stable or final but always deferred. It also demonstrates Derrida’s concern with language, difference, and the instability of presence, laying the foundation for what would soon be codified as deconstruction.

The Savage Mind 1962 Bricolage and Mythical Thought
Key Fact: Lévi-Strauss’s The Savage Mind (1962) is a seminal text that examines not only so-called “primitive” thought but also the universal structures of human cognition. The title itself contains a linguistic play: the French word pensée means both “thought” and “pansy,” while sauvage signifies “wild” rather than the English “savage.” The phrase recalls Ophelia’s line in Shakespeare’s Hamlet: “Pansies for thought” (Act IV, Scene V) . Central to this work is his introduction of the concept of bricolage , a French term meaning “tinkering” or “do-it-yourself.” Lévi-Strauss contrasted the bricoleur, who improvises solutions using available resources, with the engineer, who works systematically toward planned goals. In mythology, bricolage manifests as the re-use and recombination of existing myths and ideas to generate new narratives. This notion has since transcended anthropology, becoming a valuable interpretive tool in cultural studies, postmodern literature, and social sciences, where it denotes resourcefulness, creativity, and the capacity to construct meaning from what is already at hand. The first half of The Savage Mind outlines Lévi-Strauss’s structuralist theory of culture and cognition, while the second half expands these ideas into a broader account of history and social change. His opposition to Sartre’s existentialist notion of human freedom, and the later resonances of this debate in Pierre Bourdieu’s thought, highlight his insistence that agency is constrained and shaped by structures rather than unlimited individual choice.

In his first major work, Fearful Symmetry: A Study of William Blake (1947) , Frye reinterpreted Blake’s poetry, insisting that it should not be “deciphered” through allegorical decoding but understood within its historical and social context. He demonstrated that Blake’s visionary art created its own symbolic universe, demanding interpretation through its coherence as a system rather than through fragmentary analysis. This work established Frye as a critic capable of reconciling historical understanding with structural insight.

The defining characteristics of Romantic criticism include its celebration of nature, solitude, and the individual, alongside its focus on vivid sensory descriptions, strong emotions, and autobiographical expression. Romanticism valued imagination as a creative force capable of raising common subjects to a sublime level, moving beyond mere beauty or perfection. Its aesthetic extended into the spiritual and supernatural, often portraying nature as both awe-inspiring and deeply symbolic. Schools of Romantic poetry reflected its diversity: the Lake School of Poetry (Wordsworth, Coleridge, Southey) and the Cockney School (Leigh Hunt, Keats, Shelley) each developed distinct styles while embodying the Romantic ethos . Later critics such as M. H. Abrams, in The Mirror and the Lamp, conceptualized this critical shift: eighteenth-century literature functioned as a mirror reflecting external reality, while nineteenth-century Romanticism became a lamp, radiating inner illumination and subjective experience . In this transformation, Romantic criticism redefined literature as an art of self-expression, emotional authenticity, and imaginative power, a legacy that would profoundly shape later movements like modernism.

“Writing” (1990, in Critical Terms for Literary Study) : Johnson foregrounds the concept of écriture (writing) as central to twentieth-century French thought, exploring Roland Barthes’ adaptation of Ferdinand de Saussure’s semiotics, and how Derrida and Lacan destabilized the signifier–signified relation. She argued that reading must embrace polysemy, a plurality of meanings that opens texts to feminist and marginalized interpretations.

Johnson is remembered for her clear articulation that “Deconstruction is not synonymous with destruction… It does not proceed by random doubt… It challenges domination by one mode of signifying.” . Her approach highlighted how the indeterminacy of language—its capacity to generate contradictions and multiple meanings—could be politically enabling rather than paralyzing. By linking deconstruction to feminism, Johnson demonstrated how undecidability could open interpretive space for marginalized voices within literature.

“The Critic as Host” (1976) : Written as a reply to M.H. Abrams’ paper “The Deconstructive Angel”, this essay defends deconstruction against the accusation that critics are merely “parasites” on the plain meanings of texts. Miller inverted the metaphor, suggesting that the so-called parasite may in fact be the host, generating new insights and possibilities of meaning. Rather than a “prison-house of language,” Miller celebrated language as a “place of joy” where critics explore associations and plural readings.

Topic: The Cambridge Critics and the Foundations of New Criticism
Key Fact: The Cambridge Critics were a group of influential literary scholars at Cambridge University in the early to mid-twentieth century, whose work significantly shaped the principles of New Criticism in Britain. Key figures such as I. A. Richards, F. R. Leavis, and William Empson pioneered methods of close textual analysis that emphasized the autonomous value of the literary work. Richards, through Principles of Literary Criticism (1924) and Practical Criticism (1929), introduced a quasi-scientific approach to reading, focusing on “the words on the page” rather than historical or biographical background. Leavis, founder of Scrutiny (1932) , championed the moral and cultural role of literature, urging rigorous close reading as a means of cultivating intellectual and ethical standards. Empson’s Seven Types of Ambiguity (1930)  brought linguistic precision to the interpretation of poetry, revealing the richness and complexity of meaning. Together, the Cambridge Critics established a critical tradition that privileged form, language, and structure, laying the groundwork for modern literary analysis.

Topic: The Fugitive Group and the Rise of New Criticism in America
Key Fact: The Fugitive Group was a circle of poets, critics, and academics based at Vanderbilt University in Nashville, Tennessee, during the early 1920s, whose work played a central role in shaping American New Criticism. Named after their literary magazine The Fugitive (1922–1925) , the group included key figures such as John Crowe Ransom, Allen Tate, Robert Penn Warren, and Donald Davidson. Initially united by a shared commitment to modernist poetics and Southern cultural identity, they soon turned their attention to literary theory, advocating for close reading and formal analysis of texts. Ransom’s The New Criticism (1941)  gave the movement its name, while Tate and Warren helped popularize its methods through teaching and critical writing. The Fugitives emphasized that poetry should be studied as a self-contained aesthetic object, distinct from the author’s biography or historical context. Their influence extended beyond poetry, laying the intellectual foundation for a generation of critics who defined mid-twentieth-century American literary studies.

Topic: Scrutiny A Quarterly Review of Literature and the Arts
Key Fact: Scrutiny (1932–1953) was an influential British literary journal founded by F. R. Leavis and L. C. Knights,  dedicated to promoting rigorous moral and aesthetic standards in literary criticism. Published quarterly, the journal became the intellectual platform for the so-called "Cambridge Critics," advocating close reading, precise textual analysis, and the belief that literature should play a central role in shaping cultural and moral values. Scrutiny featured essays, reviews, and debates that addressed not only literary works but also broader issues of education, culture, and society, often challenging popular or sentimental views of literature. It championed writers like Jane Austen, George Eliot, and D. H. Lawrence, while also offering sharp re-evaluations of canonical authors. Under Leavis’s editorial guidance, Scrutiny shaped mid-20th-century British criticism by insisting on the moral seriousness of literature and resisting purely impressionistic or biographical approaches. Even after its closure in 1953, the journal’s intellectual influence continued to shape literary studies in Britain and beyond.
